does: This paper develops three distinct safety case arguments designed to address misalignment risks in powerful near-future AI systems that could engage in sabotage. Rather than presenting a complete formal safety proof, the authors sketch out plausible reasoning frameworks that could potentially rule out or substantially mitigate dangerous behaviors in advanced AI models. The work focuses on constructing concrete safety arguments that tackle specific threat vectors—particularly scenarios where capable systems might pursue deceptive or destructive actions to avoid oversight or achieve misaligned objectives. The three sketched arguments represent different approaches to safety case construction, each targeting different aspects of the misalignment problem.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature in AI safety focused on formal verification and safety cases for advanced systems. It engages with prior research on deceptive alignment, where capable models might hide their true objectives during training, and draws on debates within the AI safety community about how to credibly argue that a system will not engage in dangerous behaviors. The paper connects to broader efforts in the field to move beyond informal safety reasoning toward more structured, rigorous safety arguments—part of a lineage that includes work on interpretability, robustness verification, and formal assurance methods adapted from safety-critical systems engineering.
enables: This work points toward more systematic development of safety cases for powerful AI systems, potentially enabling clearer communication about safety guarantees to stakeholders and regulators. By sketching out the structure of plausible safety arguments, it creates a foundation for refining these arguments through continued research into interpretability, model internals, and formal verification. The paper enables future work that could build more complete and rigorous versions of these safety arguments, contributing to the infrastructure needed for responsible deployment of advanced AI systems and informing governance frameworks that might require explicit safety cases before deploying potentially dangerous capabilities.
